title: Quiz: Niederreiter Cryptosystem
description: Test your understanding of the Niederreiter variant

questions.question: How does Niederreiter encode the message?

options.text: As the positions of the error vector

questions.explanation: In Niederreiter, the message is encoded as the error pattern. A weight-t error has C(n,t) possibilities, providing enough entropy to encode messages.

questions.question: What is the ciphertext in Niederreiter?
questions.explanation: The ciphertext is the syndrome s = H'·e^T, which is (n-k) bits. This is smaller than McEliece's n-bit ciphertext.

questions.question: How does Niederreiter ciphertext size compare to McEliece?
questions.explanation: Niederreiter ciphertext is (n-k) bits (syndrome), while McEliece is n bits (codeword + error). For typical parameters, this is a significant reduction.

questions.question: Why are McEliece and Niederreiter considered equally secure?

options.text: Breaking one can be used to break the other
They have the same key size

questions.explanation: There exists a polynomial-time reduction between the two problems. An oracle that breaks one can be used to break the other, so their security is equivalent.

questions.question: What is the public key in Niederreiter?
questions.explanation: The public key is the disguised parity-check matrix H' = S·H·P, where S is scrambling and P is permutation.

questions.question: How many message bits can be encoded in a weight-t error for code length n?
questions.explanation: There are C(n,t) ways to place t errors in n positions. This provides log2(C(n,t)) bits of message capacity.

questions.question: Classic McEliece actually uses the Niederreiter formulation internally.
questions.explanation: True! The NIST submission 'Classic McEliece' uses the Niederreiter variant because it has smaller ciphertexts, despite being named after McEliece.
